raw_segments: The segment opens with sweeping aerial shots of a vast, dense forest canopy, a mix of green and golden-brown trees bathed in soft, golden light, while a calm, authoritative male narrator introduces the challenges faced by 'cats' like Scarface. The scene then transitions to an open savanna landscape where a large herd of white cattle with prominent curved horns is being herded by two cowboys on horseback, one raising a lasso, accompanied by the distinct sounds of cattle lowing, horses trotting, and a whip cracking. The narrator explains that 'Most of the Pantanal is open to cattle ranching,' and that 'Jaguar territory is filled with an unnatural, irresistible prey,' adding that while 'killing a jaguar is illegal in Brazil,' ranchers 'take revenge to protect their livelihood' when jaguars prey on livestock. A reflective acoustic guitar melody begins as the camera shifts back to the forest, following a white pickup truck driving along a narrow dirt road, its engine humming audibly. From a first-person view in the truck's open bed, Steve Winter, an older man with white hair, is seen alongside a younger Bertie Gregory. As the truck moves through the dry, sunlit forest with sparse trees, Bertie excitedly turns to the camera and exclaims, 'Check it out - there's the kill,' indicating they have discovered a recent animal kill site, a direct consequence of the jaguar's actions mentioned by the narrator, who concludes that this 'bad situation' is an 'opportunity to see how jaguars live on the fringes of the human world.' The National Geographic WILD logo remains consistently in the bottom right corner.

The segment opens with a wide shot of a dead caiman, a large armored reptile, lying motionless on a grassy patch near a dense forest. A male narrator explains that "where humans are close by, jaguars appear to be more wary of coming out in daylight," adding that "whatever killed this cow is probably waiting until nightfall to come back." The scene shifts to a white pickup truck, its engine audible, driving along a narrow dirt road through the forest. In the back, Steve Winter, an older man with white hair and a black-and-white bandana, observes, while Bertie Gregory, a younger man in a white long-sleeve shirt, gets out to set up a camera on a tripod. The narrator states, "So Steve and Bertie settle in to wait." A close-up reveals the clearly dead caiman on its side. Bertie Gregory, speaking directly to the camera with an enthusiastic British accent, shares, "Steve and I are very patient people," explaining that the "passion" and the hope of seeing "something incredible" keeps them waiting, even if they might see "nothing other than this very, very dead cow." As a golden sunset filters through the trees, dramatic, suspenseful orchestral music begins, accompanied by a low growl. The narrator announces, "As evening falls, they get a surprise. It's not the hunter that appears..." A majestic jaguar, its spotted golden-yellow coat illuminated by the warm light, emerges from the underbrush, approaches the caiman, sniffs it, and powerfully begins to drag it through the tall grass. The National Geographic WILD logo remains visible in the bottom right corner.

The video opens at dusk with a wide shot of a grassy clearing, where a large, pale caiman lies motionless on its side, belly exposed and covered in flies, accompanied by a low, suspenseful musical underscore. A large spotted jaguar emerges from tall, dry grass, identified by the narrator as 'a cub,' cautiously approaching the carcass. This is 'followed by another' second jaguar. The first jaguar sniffs the caiman, then begins to feed, tearing into the flesh with powerful bites, as the narrator reveals both jaguars are 'about 16 months old.' The camera zooms in on the jaguar’s face, showing blood around its mouth and intense focus, while the narrator explains that 'their mother made this kill.' The scene shifts to a close-up of the full moon against a dark sky, emphasizing the nocturnal setting, as the first jaguar, now understood to be the mother, rests beside the caiman with her tail curled and eyes alert, 'keeping a watchful eye as her young ones eat their fill.' As the music changes to a more mysterious tone and 'night falls over the Pantanal,' the narrator mentions 'Bertie switches to a new low-light camera.' The scene transitions to near darkness, highlighting the jaguars’ spotted coats, as the first jaguar is seen straining its muscles to drag the caiman through the grass. A second jaguar appears, approaching and interacting near the carcass, as the narrator explains that 'for the first time, he's able to see what these secretive cats get up to at night.' The two jaguars are then seen together, one lying on the caiman while the other stands guard, both appearing vigilant, with the narrator concluding that 'under the cover of darkness, the cubs relax.' The National Geographic WILD logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout the segment.

In the dim, twilight light of a dense wetland, a jaguar with a golden-yellow coat marked by black rosettes, blood visible around its mouth, approaches a large, motionless caiman lying on its side in the grass. The narrator begins, stating, "Well fed and protected by mom, they share a moment of carefree contentment," as sounds of rustling and subtle animal grunts accompany the scene. The jaguar begins to feed on the caiman, tearing at its flesh with powerful jaw movements, while distinct jaguar growls and roars intensify the primal atmosphere. A second jaguar emerges from the underbrush, approaching the first and interacting near the kill, as the narrator warns, "But they don't know this meal comes at a huge price." The two jaguars share the carcass, moving with deliberate, stealthy motions, occasionally pausing to survey their shadowy surroundings before resuming feeding or dragging the caiman slightly through the tall grasses. The narrator explains, "They're learning from their mother to hunt cows, and they're developing a taste for it," adding that "One day, this behavior may cost them their lives." Throughout, a tense, dramatic background music underscores the raw reality of predator-prey dynamics, with the National Geographic WILD logo consistently visible in the bottom right corner. The minute concludes with the narrator emphasizing the critical importance of preserved areas like the Pantanal.

event_blocks.summary: An aerial view showcases the Cuiabá River winding through the Pantanal's wetlands, characterized by dense forests and grasslands bathed in soft golden light, accompanied by gentle, slightly mysterious background music. The National Geographic WILD logo is visible as the narrator introduces the theme of jaguar survival, highlighting the absence of human threats and the abundance of natural prey within the protected borders. The scene shifts to a mother jaguar and her single cub stealthily navigating thick underbrush, their coats blending with the environment; the narrator explains the cub will mature into a formidable caiman hunter under her tutelage. The jaguar pauses, alert, before settling on a sandy riverbank with a calm, observant posture, then lounges on a fallen log near the water's edge, partially shaded and appearing relaxed. As the jaguar moves through tall grass, production credits appear, identifying Executive Producers Brian Lovett and Jeff Hasler, and Narrator Edward Marcoux. The focus shifts to a young male jaguar that has invaded Scarface's territory and retreated to the fringes, beginning to establish himself, with the narrator noting he may one day challenge Scarface but is not yet ready. The final aerial shot at dusk reveals the riverbank under a pink sky as credits continue to roll and the music softens, with the narrator concluding that the battle-scarred male jaguar maintains his rule and guardianship over the territory.

aerial landscape.description: A sweeping aerial view of the Cuiabá River winding through lush green wetlands, dense forests, and open grasslands under soft golden light, showcasing the expansive Pantanal ecosystem, described by the narrator as a place with 'no cows, no human threats, and plenty of natural prey' within its borders.

riverbank.description: A muddy, vegetated riverbank with dense green shrubs, exposed soil, and tangled roots, forming a natural border along the river where the jaguar rests and moves.

jaguar.description: A large spotted big cat with a golden-yellow coat marked by black rosettes, seen moving stealthily, resting, and lounging. The audio identifies multiple jaguars: a 'mother and her single cub,' with the cub destined to become a 'powerful caiman hunter'; a 'young male' who 'invaded Scarface's territory' and is 'beginning to find his place'; and 'Scarface,' a 'tough, battle-scarred male' who 'hangs on to his rule' and 'role as guardian.'

National Geographic WILD logo.description: A yellow rectangular logo with the word 'WILD' in white capital letters, consistently displayed in the bottom right corner of the screen throughout the segment, with no associated audio.

production credits.description: Text overlays listing names such as Executive Producers Brian Lovett and Jeff Hasler, Narrator Edward Marcoux, and other crew members, appearing over the final scenes of the jaguar walking and the aerial river view.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a sweeping aerial view of the Cuiabá River meandering through the lush, expansive wetlands of the Pantanal, showcasing dense green forests and open grasslands under soft golden light, all while gentle, slightly mysterious background music plays. The narrator immediately sets the scene as "jaguar survival," emphasizing that "inside the borders, there are no cows, no human threats, and plenty of natural prey." The National Geographic WILD logo is consistently displayed. The scene transitions to a close-up of a jaguar, identified by the narrator as a "mother and her single cub," moving stealthily through thick underbrush, its golden-yellow coat with black rosettes blending seamlessly. The narrator explains that "under her care, he will grow to become a powerful caiman hunter." The jaguar pauses, alert and watchful, before settling on a sandy riverbank, where it rests with a calm, observant demeanor. The camera then captures the jaguar lounging on a large fallen log near the river’s edge, partially shaded by overhanging branches, appearing relaxed and at ease. As the jaguar rises and walks through tall grass, production credits, including Executive Producers Brian Lovett and Jeff Hasler, and Narrator Edward Marcoux, overlay the scene. The narrator shifts focus to a "young male that invaded Scarface's territory," who "has retreated to the fringes," just "beginning to find his place." The narrator adds that "one day, he may prove a worthy adversary to Scarface, but not just yet." The final shot returns to an aerial view of the riverbank at dusk, with the sky tinged pink, as the credits continue to roll and the music softens. The narrator concludes, "For now, this tough, battle-scarred male hangs on to his rule... and to his role as guardian," underscoring the wild beauty and ongoing dynamics of the environment.

event_blocks.summary: The segment opens with an aerial view of the lush, green Pantanal wetlands and dense forest under a soft pink and orange twilight sky, as a male narrator's voice states, "of the Pantanal." Production credits for National Geographic, including Pamela Caragol and Janet Han Vissinger, overlay the scene, while an orchestral, slightly dramatic background music plays. The narrator continues, "Jaguars will care for the land, for as long as the land keeps Jaguars," over the expansive landscape. The scene then transitions to a dark underwater shot of a glowing jellyfish, followed by a close-up of a group of penguins on a rocky shore, one shaking its head and sending water droplets flying. The background music shifts to a more uplifting and epic tone. Next, a serene river flanked by grassy banks and mountains with birds flying overhead is shown, followed by a close-up of a bighorn sheep with large curved horns. An underwater shot of a shark gliding through blue water appears, and finally, a close-up of a sea turtle’s face as it swims toward the camera. Throughout these diverse wildlife and landscape shots, the National Geographic WILD logo remains in the bottom left, accompanied by 'SUBSCRIBE' and 'WATCH MORE' prompts, indicating a promotional or end-of-program segment.

penguins.description: A group of black-and-white penguins with orange beaks, standing on a rocky shore; one penguin shakes its head vigorously, spraying water droplets into the air.

jellyfish.description: A translucent, glowing jellyfish with delicate, flowing tentacles, floating in dark, deep water, illuminated from above, creating a serene and ethereal underwater scene.

bighorn sheep.description: A close-up of a brown bighorn sheep with large, curved horns, standing in a snowy or misty environment, its head turned slightly toward the camera.

sea turtle.description: A close-up of a sea turtle’s face, with dark eyes and textured skin, swimming directly toward the camera in deep blue water, surrounded by small fish.

global_entity_library.jaguar: A large spotted big cat with a golden-yellow coat marked by black rosettes, seen moving stealthily, resting, and lounging. The audio identifies multiple jaguars: a 'mother and her single cub,' with the cub destined to become a 'powerful caiman hunter'; a 'young male' who 'invaded Scarface's territory' and is 'beginning to find his place'; and 'Scarface,' a 'tough, battle-scarred male' who 'hangs on to his rule' and 'role as guardian.'
global_entity_library.caiman: A large, armored reptile with a broad snout and scaly, dark-gray skin, lying motionless on its side in the underbrush, being fed upon and dragged by two jaguars.
global_entity_library.title_card: A bold graphic overlay with the text 'JAGUAR VS. CROC' in white capital letters on a dark background, appearing during the aerial river shot, emphasizing the confrontation theme, though no crocodile is visible in this segment.
global_entity_library.aerial landscape: A sweeping aerial view of the lush, green Pantanal wetlands and dense forest under a soft pink and orange twilight sky, later transitioning to a calm river bordered by golden grasslands and rugged mountains with birds flying overhead, all while a male narrator speaks of the Pantanal and how 'Jaguars will care for the land, for as long as the land keeps Jaguars,' accompanied by an orchestral background score.
global_entity_library.capybara: A large, semi-aquatic rodent with coarse, brownish-gray fur, seen resting and sitting upright on a sandy riverbank, occasionally shifting its position, appearing calm and unaware of the approaching jaguar. It becomes the prey of the mother jaguar.
global_entity_library.satellite map: A detailed, animated satellite view of South America, zooming into Brazil and highlighting the Pantanal region, rivers, and protected parks with labeled text overlays, visually supporting the narrator's description of the Pantanal's location and protected status.
global_entity_library.howler monkey: A medium-sized primate with dark, shaggy fur and a long tail, swinging agilely through tree branches in a forested environment, heard amidst ambient nature sounds.
global_entity_library.black bird: A small, dark-colored bird with long wings, gliding low over the water’s surface in a marshy area, briefly visible in the background during the aerial shot.
global_entity_library.Steve Winter: An older man with white hair, wearing a black shirt and a black-and-white patterned bandana, seated in the back of a pickup truck, observing the surroundings with a calm demeanor. He is mentioned by the narrator as part of the team waiting, and Bertie Gregory references his words about 'passion'.
global_entity_library.Bertie Gregory: A younger man with light brown hair, wearing a white long-sleeve shirt and sunglasses perched on his head. He is initially seen setting up a camera on a tripod and later speaks directly to the camera with an enthusiastic British accent, explaining their patience and referring to the dead caiman as a 'very, very dead cow'.
global_entity_library.macaw: A vibrant blue-and-yellow parrot perched in a palm tree, holding and eating a piece of fruit with its strong beak, surrounded by green fronds, showcasing the region’s rich avian life.
global_entity_library.boat: A motorboat labeled 'Alufort' with a canopy, navigating a muddy river, equipped with a radio and other gear, carrying Steve Winter and the driver. The narrator states that Bertie and Steve, observing from the boat, 'have also gotten their reward' by witnessing the jaguar's hunt.
global_entity_library.riverbank: A muddy, vegetated riverbank with dense green shrubs, exposed soil, and tangled roots, forming a natural border along the river where the jaguar rests and moves.
global_entity_library.second jaguar: A second large spotted big cat with a golden-yellow coat marked by black rosettes, approaching the first jaguar, sharing the caiman carcass, and standing guard with alert posture. Its actions are accompanied by the sounds of jaguar growls and roars, and the narration implies it is a young animal, possibly a cub, learning to hunt from its mother.
global_entity_library.camera_operator: A person wearing a blue shirt, crouched behind a professional camera rig on a boat, actively filming the jaguars from a concealed position near the riverbank, with the river and greenery visible in the background.
global_entity_library.bird: A small bird with a yellow belly and grayish-brown back, perched on the capybara’s back, pecking at its fur, likely removing parasites, and remaining calm as the capybara moves through the grassy area.
global_entity_library.National Geographic WILD logo: A yellow rectangular logo with the word 'WILD' in white capital letters, consistently displayed in the bottom left corner of the screen throughout the segment, accompanied by 'SUBSCRIBE' and 'WATCH MORE' text.
global_entity_library.otter: Dark, wet, playful animals seen splashing and interacting in the water. A male narrator describes them as 'much better swimmers' that can 'attack as fiercely as a pack of dogs,' while Bertie Gregory refers to them as 'giant otters' and 'one of the few animals here that can put up a pretty good fight.'

global_entity_library.stork: A black-headed stork with a red throat and white body, standing in shallow water and spreading its wings in a close-up shot, identified by Bertie Gregory as a 'Jabiru stork' and described by him as 'absolutely massive'.
